coredata.dc:title: A multichannel single-well sensor array for rapid and visual discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters
coredata.dc:creator: Abbasi-Moayed, Samira
Hormozi-Nezhad, M. Reza
Maaza, Malik
coredata.dc:description: In this study, for rapid identification and discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters in the presence of ascorbic acid (AA), a tricolor fluorescence probe has been constructed by integrating three recognition elements including blue carbon dots (bCDs), green CdTe quantum dots (gQDs) and red CdTe quantum dots (rQDs) into a single well. The proposed array exhibited ternary fluorescence emissions at 450, 520 and 630 nm with an overall luminescent pink emission under a single wavelength excitation (365 nm). In order to produce distinct response patterns from a single fluorimetric test, the spectral changes and the corresponding color variations of the multichannel fluorescence probe were monitored. Finally, analyzing the response profiles with pattern recognition methods allowed us to identify and discriminate catecholamine neurotransmitters and to also distinguish them from AA which is known as a serious interference in urine samples. Altogether, the developed sensor array can be considered as a rapid and visual tool for diagnostic and therapeutic applications in the near future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tricolor fluorescence probe
Sensor array
Naked-eye discrimination
Catecholamine neurotransmitters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-Acetyl L-Cysteine capped CdTe QDs
Fabrication of the multichannel single-well fluorescence probe
Responses of the single-well fluorescence probe
Identification of catecholamine neurotransmitters in urine sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science, Research and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to express their gratitude to Sharif University of Technology, the South African National Research Foundation (NRF) and the Ministry of Science, Research and Technology of the Islamic Republic of Iran (MSRT) for their support of this work. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of the multichannel fluorescence probe design based on simple combination of bCDs, gQDs and rQDs for the discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA in a quick single fluorometric test.
Structures of a) Dopamine b) Norepinephrine c) L-dopa d) ascorbic acid their oxidation products.
Florescent spectra of gQDs, rQDs, bCDs and the tricolor fluorescence probe at an excitation of 365 nm (inset: their corresponding photograph taken under a 365 nm UV lamp).

(A) Florescent spectra of the multichannel fluorescence probe before and after exposure to DA, NE, LD and AA in the concentration range of 5–100 μM, (B) Visual images of the tricolor fluorescence probe in the presence of DA, NE, LD and AA (40–80 μM).

(A) HCA dendrogram of catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA at concentration range of 30–100 μM using Ward’s linkage. (B) Two-dimensional canonical score plot for the discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters in the presence of AA at concentration range of 10–100 μM.

(A) Florescence spectra (B) image of single-well tricolor fluorescence probe in the presence of pure and mixtures of catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA. (C) Two-dimensional canonical score plot for the discrimination of individual catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA from their mixtures. (The concentration of each analyte was 60 μM in pure samples, 30 μM in binary mixtures, 20 μM in ternary mixtures and 15 in quaternary mixtures).

(A) Florescent spectra (B) image of single-well tricolor fluorescence probe in the presence of possible interference species. (The concentration of analytes was 60 μM and the concentration of interferences was 300 μM).
Two-dimensional LDA score plot after combining the test set (real sample) with the training set.

head.ce:title: A multichannel single-well sensor array for rapid and visual discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, 11155-9516, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, 11155-9516, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, 11155-9516, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, 11155-9516, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UNESCO-UNISA Africa Chair in Nanosciences/Nanotechnology Laboratories, College of Graduate Studies, University of South Africa (UNISA), Muckleneuk Ridge, P.O. Box 392, Pretoria, South Africa

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: UNESCO-UNISA Africa Chair in Nanosciences/Nanotechnology Laboratories

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: UNESCO-UNISA Africa Chair in Nanosciences/Nanotechnology Laboratories, College of Graduate Studies, University of South Africa (UNISA), Muckleneuk Ridge, P O Box 392, Pretoria, South Africa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanosciences African network (NANOAFNET), Materials Research Group (MRG), iThemba LABS-National Research Foundation (NRF), 1 Old Faure Road, 7129, P.O. Box 722, Somerset West, Western Cape Province, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanosciences African network (NANOAFNET), Materials Research Group (MRG), iThemba LABS-National Research Foundation (NRF), 1 Old Faure Road, 7129, P O Box 722, Somerset West, Western Cape Province, South Africa

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Chemistry Department, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, 11155-9516, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, for rapid identification and discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters in the presence of ascorbic acid (AA), a tricolor fluorescence probe has been constructed by integrating three recognition elements including blue carbon dots (bCDs), green CdTe quantum dots (gQDs) and red CdTe quantum dots (rQDs) into a single well. The proposed array exhibited ternary fluorescence emissions at 450, 520 and 630 nm with an overall luminescent pink emission under a single wavelength excitation (365 nm). In order to produce distinct response patterns from a single fluorimetric test, the spectral changes and the corresponding color variations of the multichannel fluorescence probe were monitored. Finally, analyzing the response profiles with pattern recognition methods allowed us to identify and discriminate catecholamine neurotransmitters and to also distinguish them from AA which is known as a serious interference in urine samples. Altogether, the developed sensor array can be considered as a rapid and visual tool for diagnostic and therapeutic applications in the near future.

ce:section.ce:para: Catecholamine neurotransmitters (e.g. dopamine (DA), norepinephrine (NE) and levodopa (LD)) with a catechol structure play crucial roles in early diagnosis of different diseases such as Parkinsonism, Schizophrenia, Alzheimer, Down’s syndrome, severe head trauma, neuroblastomas, pheochromocytomas and multiple sclerosis [1–5]. Several methods including electrochemical [6,7], optical (e.g., flourimetric and colorimetric methods) [8–10], chromatography (gas chromatography, liquid chromatography) [11,12] and mass spectrometry [13–15] have been reported so far for their detection. However, these methods usually face some challenges such as time-consuming procedures and high costs associated with complicated instrumentation. In addition, the similar electroactive behavior of catecholamine neurotransmitters as well as their similarity with other species such as AA causes a serious limitation for electrochemical determination of these compounds [16–18]. Therefore, the development of simple, quick and efficient methods for catecholamine neurotransmitters sensing in the presence of possible interferences, mainly ascorbic acid, is highly demanded.

Optical sensor array which is also known as the optoelectronic tongue/nose, is a promising sensing approach for the recognition and discrimination of groups of target species [19,20]. Contrary to the traditional “lock-and-key” approach, in the chemical tongue system, cross-reactive sensor elements are utilized to generate distinct patterns for each analyte [21]. Over the past few years, nanoparticles (NPs) have been used as efficient array-based sensing elements [22,23] for the discrimination of various analytes such as biomolecules [24–32], explosives [33–35], pestisides [36,37] and metal ions [38–40], which is due to their interesting tunable physicochemical features. More recently, among the different types of NPs available, quantum dots (QDs) have attracted significant attention in array based sensors because of their high emission quantum yields, size-tunable and narrow emissions [41]. Interestingly, simple mixing of QDs with other fluorophores such as CDs or organic dyes promotes the design of RGB (red, green, and blue)-based sensor arrays for visual discrimination of analytes [42,43]. However, it must be noted that in most common array based approaches, one should investigate the interaction of each sensing element with all the analytes individually, suffering from too many time consuming measurements as well as the associated high cost experiments. Thus, by designing single well arrays in which different nanostructure based sensor elements are mixed together, a promising approach will be achieved for the identification and discrimination of target analytes. These single well array designs which are based on quick single fluorimetric runs, hold great promise in naked-eye sensing approaches [33,44].

Herein, we introduce a multichannel fluorescence sensor array for the identification and discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters. The tricolor fluorescence probe was prepared by simply mixing blue CDs (bCDs), green CdTe QDs (gQDs) and red CdTe QDs (rQDs) which exhibit ternary fluorescence emissions at respectively, 450, 520 and 630 nm under a single wavelength excitation (365 nm). After the addition of different catecholamine neurotransmitters, the three fluorescence emission bands of the multiplexed probe changed differentially, giving rise to fingerprint patterns for each analyte. The unique colorful response patterns were achieved with a single fluorimetric test and were identifiable by naked eye. Pattern recognition techniques including hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) and linear discriminant analysis (LDA) were employed for data analysis and discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters. Although a number of NP-based sensor arrays have previously described the discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters [24,25], there are no reports on the naked-eye discrimination of these compounds in the presence of AA with a single fluorimetric test.

Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), tellurium powder (Te), cadmium chloride dihydrate (CdCl2.2H2O), N-Acetyl L-Cysteine, dopamine hydrochloride (DA), norepinephrine hydrochloride (NE), L-DOPA (LD) and ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. urea, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were provided from Merck. Milli-Q grade water, with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ, was utilized during all the experiments.

Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer was used for fluorescence spectra recording. A Samsung Galaxy S7 smartphone was utilized for collecting photographic images, in manual mode, autofocus; at ISO 100 and a shutter speed of 1/180 s. The measurements of pH were recorded using a Denver Instrument Model of 270 pH meter equipped with 22 a Metrohm glass electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-Acetyl L-Cysteine capped CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: N-Acetyl L-Cysteine capped CdTe QDs with different sizes which show diverse emission colors were prepared based on a previously reported approach with slight modifications [45]. In summary, 2.87 mmol of N-Acetyl L-Cysteine was added to 10 mL of 0.290 M CdCl2 solution; the pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to 9.0 by NaOH solution. NaBH4 (75 mL, 64 mM) was stirred under argon flow for 20 min, and then Te powder (0.76 mmol) was added. After 20 min, 10 mL of the pH-adjusted CdCl2 solution which was stirred under argon flow for 5 min, was added to the above solution, followed by increasing the temperature to 150℃. N-Acetyl L-Cysteine capped CdTe QDs with different sizes was achieved at diverse reaction times and was allowed to cool in ice bath. The as-prepared green and red CdTe QDs, exhibited excellent emission bands at 520 and 630 nm, correspondingly.

Photoluminescent CDs were synthesized according to the method reported previously based on a hydrothermal route. Briefly, 0.3 g citric acid and 0.3 g Urea were mixed together in water (10 mL). The mixture solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teﬂon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the multichannel single-well fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The tricolor fluorescence probe was achieved by simply mixing 1000 μL rQDs (100 times diluted), 350 μL gQDs (10 times diluted), 60 μL bCDs (1000 times diluted) and 180 μL NaOH (1 M) and the mixture was incubated for 10 min. The concentration of NaOH was 60 mM. 100 μL of different concentrations of catecholamine neurotransmitters were added to the developed probe and the fluorescence spectra and corresponding images were recorded 15 min thereafter at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm.
The urine sample which was attained from a healthy volunteer, was diluted 50 times with Milli-Q water and then the analytes were spiked to it.

All the analyses including spectra recording or image capturing were conducted in triplicates. Chemometric analysis were carried out on the data matrix (based on spectra or RGB outputs) using SYSTAT (version 13.0) and Origin pro 2017 (version 9.4). All of the ellipses meet 90% confidence ellipses in the 2D canonical score plots.

Monitoring color variations in naked-eye based detections is a very useful and attractive way for identifying different analytes. For this purpose, as shown in Scheme 1 , a multichannel fluorescence probe was developed based on simply mixing bCDs, gQDs and rQDs together, allowing the discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters in a quick single fluorimetric test. As shown in Fig. 1 , the tricolor fluorescence probe showed ternary-emission bands at 450, 520 and 630 nm under a single wavelength excitation (365 nm) and exhibited a pink fluorescence emission color. bCDs, gQDs and rQDs, as sensor elements, were combined in single-well for to quickly discriminate the target analytes within a single fluorescence run. In the presence of sufficient oxidizing agents or adequate quantities of alkali, catecholamine neurotransmitters can be oxidized and form polymeric products which are fluorescent [47,48]. The oxidation reactions of three catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA are shown in Scheme 2 . Taking use of the differential interaction between the oxidized products and the green and red QDs via inner filter effect or electron transfer (ET) between QDs and quinones, [49–51], we designed a single-well sensor array for the discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters, most importantly in the presence of AA which due to its similar structure, is known as a serious interference in urine samples.

In order to improve the sensitivity and the performance of the proposed multichannel fluorescence probe in the identification and discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters in the presence of AA, some important parameters such as NaOH concentration and time were optimized.

According to previous studies, the concentration of NaOH as the oxidizing agent has a vital role on the oxidation of different catecholamine neurotransmitters [25,52]. For example, the oxidation products of NE and LD in the presence of adequate amounts of NaOH show fluorescence emissions around 500and 450 nm, respectively [53,54]. Moreover, it has been reported that the oxidation products of catecholamine neurotransmitters may quench the fluorescence emission of QDs [25,55]. Therefore, we found it important to investigate the effect of NaOH concentration as a pivotal parameter effecting the discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters. Fig. S1 shows the variations in I-I0/I0 (I: fluorescence intensity in the presence of analyte, I0: fluorescence intensity in the absence of analytes) at three wavelengths (450, 520 and 630 nm) vs NaOH concentration for all the analytes. The fluorescence intensity of the oxidation product of LD (450 nm) and NE (500 nm) increased with increasing NaOH concentration. However, the results revealed that the quenching in QDs emission was raised by increasing the concentration of NaOH up to 80 mM which was due to the instability of QDs at high NaOH concentrations. Moreover, by comparing the LDA score plots of the discrimination of analytes at two NaOH concentrations (40 and 60 mM), the concentration of 60 mM was chosen as the optimum concentration for all further experiments (Fig. S2).

The fluorescence responses of the probe were monitored for 20 min in the presence of the analytes to find the optimum time for discrimination. As shown in Fig. S3, the responses were completed at 15 min, hence the discrimination was achieved at this time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responses of the single-well fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of the probe in the presence of various catecholamine neurotransmitters (DA, NE and LD) and AA were recorded in a concentration range of 5–100 μM and are demonstrated in Fig. 2 A. As can be clearly seen, a unique spectral pattern was provided for each catecholamine neurotransmitters or AA within a single fluorimetric test. As shown in Fig. S4, compared with NE and LD which show fluorescence emission at about 500 and 450 respectively, the fluorescence emission of the polymeric product of DA was negligible. This can be for the fact that the cyclization reaction for DA was insignificant and the back reaction can occur due to its different functional group [53]. Moreover, the oxidation product of AA doesn’t also show fluorescence peak because it cannot produce the polymeric product. Therefore, with the addition of LD and NE, the fluorescence intensity of bCDs and gQDs were increased respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence of CdTe QDs can be quenched in the presence of oxidation product of catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA due to inner filter effect or electron transfer (ET) between QDs and quinones. The quinones as electron acceptor can approach to the surface of QDs through a different functional group of catecholamine neurotransmitters; the fluorescence of QDs were quenched with electron transferring from QDs to quinones. Thus, the fluorescence of gQDs and rQDs quenched in the presence of DA, LD or AA differently due to their different functional groups. The fluorescence of rQDs also quenched in the presence of NE. The fluorescence intensity of bCDs don’t show any significant change in the presence of DA, NE or AA which can be exploited as a beneficial phenomenon for developing visual probe. In addition, the color variations of the tricolor fluorescence probe in the presence of 40–80 μM of each analyte were recorded using the camera of a smartphone (Fig. 2B). Fig. S5 shows the RGB based responses (ΔR, ΔG and ΔB) which were calculated corresponding to different concentrations (40–80 μM) of the target analytes. These RGB based responses also show a distinct pattern for each neurotransmitter and for AA.

The potential of the proposed multichannel fluorescence probe for the discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters as well as their discrimination from AA as a serious interference, was assessed by pattern recognition methods. The response signals of the sensor (I-I0/I0) at three wavelengths (450, 520 and 630 nm, each related to one of the sensor elements), were chosen as the data matrix for further analysis. HCA as an unsupervised method was used to cluster the target analytes according to the distance (Euclidean distance) between their data points. Fig. 3 A shows that HCA successfully clustered the catecholamine nuerotransmitters and AA in four distinct groups within the concentration range of 30–100 μM, based on the minimum variance (Ward’s) method. LDA as a supervised method was applied to classify the data by finding linear combinations of the initial variables (sensor elements) with the aim of maximizing the between-class variance and minimizing the within-class variance. In this method, after building the model, an analyte with an unknown class can be assigned to one of the predefined classes based on the similarity between their responses. Owing to its prediction ability, LDA was employed on the training matrix (3 wavelength (sensor elements) × 4 analytes × 9 concentration × 3 replicate) and transformed the initial recorded emission signals to the new space defined by canonical factors in which the data were classified into four distinct groups. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, two canonical factors (68.64% and 12.43%) were depicted in a two-dimensional plot in which the target analytes with different concentration (10–100 μM) were classified into four distinct groups (Table. S1). Since a critical step in designing array-based sensors is to validate the robustness of the LDA classifier, we performed with leave many out cross-validation to test the predictability of our single-well sensor array. Table S2 shows a 98% accuracy for the identification of unknown samples taken out from the training set. This reveals the reproducibility of the proposed multichannel fluorescence probe in the identification of catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA. Furthermore, the capability of our probe for visual discrimination of target analytes was examined by performing LDA on RGB based responses. Fig. S6 confirms the visual discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters in the presence of AA in the concentration range of 40–80 μM.

In order to assess the quantification ability of the developed fluorescence probe, the total Euclidean distances (TED) of the overall sensor array’s response (i.e. square root of the sums of squares of the sensor elements’ responses) vs the concentration of catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA was depicted as shown in Fig. S7. Linear ranges were detected in the range of 5–50 μM for DA, 10–80 μM for NE, 5–60 μM for LD and 20–100 μM for AA accordingly. The limits of detection were found to be 2.7, 3.3, 8.3 and 17.8 μM for DA, NE, LD and AA, respectively.

The ability of sensor arrays in discriminating individual analytes from their mixtures is one of their most important advantages. In this regard, the response signals of the proposed probe upon the addition of mixtures of catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA (the total concentration for all mixtures was 60 μM) were recorded. As shown in Fig. 4 , mixtures of catecholamine neurotransmitters had distinct responses, clearly distinguishable from their pure counterparts. The LDA score plot demonstrates that all mixtures as well as individual analytes were fully discriminated with 100% cross-validation accuracy.

To investigate the feasibility of the multichannel fluorescence probe for the discrimination of analytes in practical applications, the fluorescence responses were recorded in the presence of aromatic amino acids which possess similar structures to catecholamine neurotransmitters (i.e. tryptophan, tyrosine, phenylalanine, histidine) and some urine ingredients (i.e. urea, uric acid, Na+, K+ and Ca2+). As shown in Fig. 5 , no significant changes were observed in the fluorescence profiles or the color emissions of the probe upon the addition of interferences species. The results demonstrated that these species did not affect the discrimination of our target analytes (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of catecholamine neurotransmitters in urine sample
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, the applicability of the proposed multichannel fluorescence probe for identification and discrimination of target analytes in urine sample was evaluated. The fluorescence responses of the probe to spiked urine samples (60 μM of catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA) are shown in Fig. S9. LDA was further applied on data matrixes consisting both the training set and a set of unknown catecholamine neurotransmitters in urine sample, as the test set. The results confirmed that the analytes in urine sample were correctly assigned to their own class with 96.3% accuracy (Fig. 6 , Table S3). Furthermore, there is no significant change in the fluorescence responses of the multichannel probe in the presence of interferences species in a urine sample (Fig. S10). Thus as shown in Fig. S11, the discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA in urine sample were achieved in the presence of the interference species.

We have fabricated a multichannel fluorescence probe by mixing together three sensor elements (bCDs, gQDs and rQDs) to visually discriminate catecholamine neurotransmitters and AA by a single fluorometric test. The color emission of the proposed tricolor fluorescence probe changed differently after the addition of each target analyte, producing a distinct fingerprint pattern for its identification. Moreover, the single-well fluorescence probe demonstrated excellent performance in visual discrimination of catecholamine neurotransmitters in the presence of AA at different concentrations. This single-well tricolor fluorescence probe can be considered as an efficient biomarker sensing approach for diagnostic and therapeutic applications in future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their gratitude to Sharif University of Technology, the South African National Research Foundation (NRF) and the Ministry of Science, Research and Technology of the Islamic Republic of Iran (MSRT) for their support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.126691.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanoparticles-based barcode analysis for detection of norepinephrine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Hee An, K.-J. Lee, J.-W. Choi, Gold nanoparticles-based barcode analysis for detection of norepinephrine, Journal of biomedical nanotechnology, 12 (2016) 357-365.

Lin, A unique turn-off fluorescent strategy for sensing dopamine based on formed polydopamine (pDA) using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as fluorescent probe, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 221 (2015) 7-14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-signal microbial biosensor for the detection of dopamine without inference from other catecholamine neurotransmitters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-K. Lin, Y.-C. Yeh, Dual-signal microbial biosensor for the detection of dopamine without inference from other catecholamine neurotransmitters, Anal. Chem., 89 (2017) 11178-11182.

Hubert, Performances of a multidimensional on-line SPE-LC-ECD method for the determination of three major catecholamines in native human urine: Validation, risk and uncertainty assessments, J. Chromatogr. B, 844 (2006) 251-260.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Leptin regulates dopamine responses to sustained stress in humans

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.R. Burghardt, T.M. Love, C.S. Stohler, C. Hodgkinson, P.-H. Shen, M.-A. Enoch, D. Goldman, J.-K. Zubieta, Leptin regulates dopamine responses to sustained stress in humans, J. Neurosci., 32 (2012) 15369-15376.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel modifications to carbon-based electrodes to improve the electrochemical detection of dopamine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Jiang, G.W. Nelson, J. Abda, J.S. Foord, Novel modifications to carbon-based electrodes to improve the electrochemical detection of dopamine, ACS applied materials & interfaces, 8 (2016) 28338-28348.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent biosensor for the selective determination of dopamine by TGA-capped CdTe quantum dots in human plasma samples

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoprobe for sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of dopamine via robust Ag–catechol interaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Lin, C. Chen, C. Wang, F. Pu, J. Ren, X. Qu, Silver nanoprobe for sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of dopamine via robust Ag–catechol interaction, Chem. Commun., 47 (2011) 1181-1183.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dopamine-, L-DOPA-, adrenaline-, and noradrenaline-induced growth of Au nanoparticles: assays for the detection of neurotransmitters and of tyrosinase activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Baron, M. Zayats, I. Willner, Dopamine-, L-DOPA-, adrenaline-, and noradrenaline-induced growth of Au nanoparticles: assays for the detection of neurotransmitters and of tyrosinase activity, Anal. Chem., 77 (2005) 1566-1571.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of selected neurotransmitter metabolites using monolithic column chromatography coupled with chemiluminescence detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.L. Adcock, N.W. Barnett, J.W. Costin, P.S. Francis, S.W. Lewis, Determination of selected neurotransmitter metabolites using monolithic column chromatography coupled with chemiluminescence detection, Talanta, 67 (2005) 585-589.

Chromatogr. B, 807 (2004) 177-183.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development and validation of an ultra-high performance liquid chromatography–tandem mass-spectrometry (UHPLC–MS/MS) method for the simultaneous determination of neurotransmitters in rat brain samples

Pérez, Development and validation of an ultra-high performance liquid chromatography–tandem mass-spectrometry (UHPLC–MS/MS) method for the simultaneous determination of neurotransmitters in rat brain samples, Journal of neuroscience methods, 198 (2011) 187-194.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry method for determination of neurotransmitters in brain tissue: application to human tauopathies
